metadata.summary: Designing three-pillar observability (metrics, logs, traces) so engineers can answer why systems fail. SLI/SLO/error-budget, OpenTelemetry, sampling, alerting, and cost optimization.

content.title: Observability Architecture
content.summary: Designing the three-pillar observability stack (metrics, logs, traces) so engineers can answer "why is this broken?" not just "is it broken?". Covers SLI/SLO/error-budget instrumentation, OpenTelemetry collector pipelines, sampling strategies, alerting on burn rate, and cost-optimisation through tiered retention and tail sampling.

why.p: Monitoring tells you when something fails; observability tells you why. Without correlated metrics + logs + traces you cannot answer "why does p99 latency spike for users in EU on Friday afternoons?" Semantic tagging (trace_id on every log line) enables root-cause analysis without pre-configuring every dashboard upfront — critical for unknown failure modes.

list.item: Instrumenting a new service before production launch
Migrating from ad-hoc logging to structured, correlated telemetry
Implementing SLO-based alerting (multi-burn-rate alerts)
Reducing observability costs by replacing head sampling with tail sampling
Designing OpenTelemetry Collector pipelines for Kubernetes workloads
A single-process script or cron job — stdout logging is sufficient
When the existing stack already covers the failure surface and cost is a constraint
Before SLOs are defined — observability investment without SLOs produces dashboards nobody acts on

rule.statement: Instrument all three pillars (metrics, logs, traces) and correlate them with shared identifiers (trace_id, span_id) to enable root-cause analysis of unknown failure modes.
rule.rationale: Metrics reveal that p99 latency spiked; logs show the error message; traces pinpoint which downstream service introduced the latency. Without all three correlated, root cause requires guesswork.

list.item: Metrics: aggregate numerical data over time (counter, gauge, histogram, summary). Answers "what happened and how often?"
Logs: discrete events with timestamp and context. Answers "why did it happen?"
Traces: end-to-end request paths across services. Answers "where in the call chain did it happen?"

section.name: Instrumentation Frameworks
section.p: RED Method (for request-handling services): Rate (req/s), Errors (failed req/s), Duration (latency distribution). Apply to every service endpoint.
USE Method (for resources): Utilisation (% busy), Saturation (queue depth), Errors (error count). Apply to CPU, memory, disk, network, connection pools.
Golden Signals (Google SRE): Latency, Traffic, Errors, Saturation. Superset of RED; use at service dashboard level.

rule.statement: Base alerts on SLO burn rate, not raw metric thresholds.
rule.rationale: A 5% error rate at 1 req/min consumes trivial error budget; a 0.5% error rate at 100k req/min exhausts the monthly budget in hours. Burn rate alerts catch both scenarios with a single rule.

section.name: SLI and Error Budget

rule.statement: Define 2-3 SLIs per critical service; reject SLIs that cannot be measured from the user's perspective.
rule.rationale: More than 3 SLIs per service creates alert fatigue and makes error budget policy ambiguous. Internal queue depth is not an SLI unless users directly observe it.

section.p: Error budget table: 99% SLO = 7.3 h/month; 99.9% = 43 min/month; 99.95% = 21.6 min/month; 99.99% = 4.3 min/month.

list.item: Budget consumed 0-50%: normal development velocity
Budget consumed 50-75%: prioritise reliability tickets this sprint
Budget consumed 75-100%: reliability-focused sprint only
Budget consumed 100%+: feature freeze until budget recovers

section.name: OpenTelemetry Standard

rule.statement: Use OpenTelemetry SDK as the single instrumentation layer for metrics, logs, and traces; avoid vendor-specific SDKs in application code.
rule.rationale: Vendor-specific instrumentation creates lock-in: switching backends requires rewriting all instrumentation. OTel is CNCF standard with 76% adoption (2025); backends can be swapped by changing Collector exporters only.

section.p: Architecture: Application + OTel SDK → OTel Collector (gateway) → Mimir (metrics) + Loki (logs) + Tempo (traces) → Grafana (visualisation).

rule.statement: Emit JSON-structured logs with trace_id and span_id on every log line to enable log-to-trace correlation.
rule.rationale: Without trace_id on logs, correlating a log error to a specific trace requires timestamp matching — unreliable at high throughput. Shared trace_id enables one-click pivot from log to trace in Grafana.

rule.statement: Use ERROR level only for failures requiring human action; use WARN for handled degradation; use INFO for normal business events.
rule.rationale: Alert rules fire on ERROR log rate. If ERROR is used loosely (e.g., 404 responses), alert thresholds must be raised, masking real errors.

section.name: Trace Context Propagation

rule.statement: Use W3C Trace Context (traceparent header) as the propagation format for HTTP and message queue boundaries.
rule.rationale: W3C Trace Context is the IETF standard; mixing formats (B3, Jaeger) across services breaks trace stitching at service boundaries.

section.p: Propagate through: HTTP headers (traceparent), Kafka message headers, async task metadata. Verify propagation in integration tests — missing propagation silently breaks distributed traces.

rule.statement: Apply tail-based sampling: always sample 100% of error traces and traces exceeding the p99 latency threshold; apply 1-5% probabilistic sampling for the remainder.
rule.rationale: Head sampling (random % at trace start) discards error traces at the same rate as successful ones — the opposite of what debugging needs. Tail sampling waits for the full trace before deciding.

list.item: Always sample: status=ERROR, latency above p99 threshold, critical user paths
Never sample: health check endpoints (/health/live, /health/ready)
Probabilistic: 1-5% of all other traces

rule.statement: Alert on burn rate at two windows (short 5m/1h for fast burn; long 30m/6h for slow burn) rather than on raw error rate thresholds.
rule.rationale: A raw 1% error rate alert fires regardless of budget impact. A 14.4x burn rate alert (consuming 1 month of budget in 2 hours) fires only when the situation is urgent. Two-window approach reduces false positives from transient spikes.

example.note: Critical: 14.4x burn rate sustained over 5m AND 1h (page immediately). Warning: 6x burn rate sustained over 30m AND 6h (review within hours). Both windows must fire to avoid false positives.

rule.statement: Every alert must have a runbook URL in its annotation and a unique, human-readable summary.
rule.rationale: Alerts without runbooks require on-call engineers to diagnose from scratch under pressure. Runbook link reduces MTTR by providing diagnosis and mitigation steps immediately.

list.item: Critical: page immediately (any time) → PagerDuty. Example: service down, SLO breached.
Warning: review within hours → Slack. Example: elevated error rate, disk filling.
Info: check during business hours → email/ticket. Example: unusual pattern, approaching limit.

rule.statement: Configure alert inhibition so that a critical alert suppresses related warning alerts on the same service.
rule.rationale: When a service is down (critical), related warning alerts for the same service create noise that distracts the responder from the root issue.

section.name: Cost Optimisation

rule.statement: Limit metric label cardinality to fewer than 10 unique values per label to prevent series explosion.
rule.rationale: A label with user_id as value on a high-traffic service creates millions of unique time series, causing storage and query performance to collapse. Use bucketed or categorical labels only.

section.p: Cost reduction strategies: tail sampling (70-90% trace volume reduction), log aggregation at collector (30-50% storage), cardinality limits on high-volume metrics (40-60% series reduction), tiered retention (50-70% storage reduction).

list.item: Hot tier (7-14 days): active debugging, fast queries
Warm tier (30-90 days): recent incident analysis
Cold tier (1-2 years): compliance, audits, object storage
Archive (3+ years): legal requirements, cheapest storage class

antipattern.description: Storing all traces at 100% sample rate without tail sampling or retention tiers.
antipattern.reason: At 10k req/s, 100% trace storage can cost 10-50x more than a 5% probabilistic + error-always strategy, with no diagnostic benefit for the 95% of successful traces.

template.purpose: Production OTel Collector config with tail sampling, batch, memory limiter, and LGTM exporters
Multi-burn-rate SLO alerting rules + infrastructure/K8s recording rules
FastAPI + OpenTelemetry SDK setup with trace context injection into structured logs
